description: This test case evaluates the RAG system's robustness in retrieving a specific, rare, or hard-to-find fact (the 'needle') from a large context ('haystack').
question: Explain how the system described in GB2627014_GB2627014A manages character recognition on damaged or fragmented characters imprinted on panels or packaged modules, including the strategies used to handle local thresholding and image binarization.

gold_context_sources.evidence: The character recognition system is configured to obtain a digital image of a character imprinted on a surface of the panel, the character recognition system being further configured to: perform image analysis on the digital image of the character, the image analysis including applying a local threshold value to one or more localities of the digital image to provide a binarized image of the character; and classify the character based on the image analysis, the local threshold value applied to each locality of the one or more localities being based on pixel information around each respective locality. ... The character recognition system is further configured to apply a fragmented search region to the digital image to obtain less than a whole of the character and apply the image analysis to the less than a whole of the character. ... The character recognition system is further configured to determine the fragmented search region based on analysis of damage to the character. ... If the character cannot successfully be classified based on the image analysis, the character recognition system is further configured to apply a global threshold value to the binarized digital image to provide a binarized digital image of the character. ... The character recognition system is further configured to iteratively apply global threshold values to provide a series of binarized digital images of the character, each iteration having a different global threshold value, until the character can be successfully classified.

ground_truth_answer: The system described in GB2627014_GB2627014A performs character recognition on characters imprinted on panels or packaged modules, even when the characters are damaged or fragmented. It achieves this by first obtaining a digital image of the character and then performing image analysis that includes applying local threshold values to multiple localities within the image. This local thresholding is based on pixel information surrounding each locality, enabling adaptive binarization tailored to different parts of the character image. To handle damaged or fragmented characters, the system applies a fragmented search region approach that isolates parts of the character, excluding damaged areas from the analysis. This fragmented region is determined based on an analysis of damage to the character, effectively focusing recognition efforts on intact portions. If initial classification attempts fail, the system employs a fallback mechanism by applying global threshold values iteratively to the binarized image, generating a series of binarized images with varying thresholds until successful classification is achieved. This combination of local thresholding, fragmented region analysis, and iterative global thresholding enhances the robustness and accuracy of character recognition in challenging conditions involving partial damage or fragmentation.
